perturbation.original_text: To maintain the Information in the strictest of confidence and to control the dissemination of the Information, including any documents or copies (paper, electronic or otherwise) thereof contained in the Information in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Confidentiality and Non- Disclosure Agreement (“ NDA”);
perturbation.changed_text: To maintain the Information in confidence and to control the dissemination of the Information, including any documents or copies thereof; this obligation does not extend to disclosures required by law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to 'strictest' confidence and adding an exception for legally required disclosures, the confidentiality obligation is weakened and may not meet the standard required under certain regulations, potentially creating a loophole.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality which could potentially conflict with the Economic Espionage Act if the 'Information' constitutes a trade secret. The clause 'this obligation does not extend to disclosures required by law' provides an overly broad exception that could allow for disclosures not genuinely mandated by law, thereby undermining protection of trade secrets.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 90 / § 1832 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1832 : Theft of trade secrets Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 90-PROTECTION OF TRADE SECRETS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments §1832. Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligation, potentially conflicting with the Economic Espionage Act if the 'Information' constitutes a trade secret. The original text states that information will be held in the 'strictest of confidence.' The changed text removes 'strictest of confidence' and includes 'this obligation does not extend to disclosures required by law,' which creates a broad exception. According to the Law Explanation, this exception could allow disclosures not genuinely mandated by law. Snippet 2 references 18 U.S.C. § 1832, Theft of Trade Secrets, which makes it a crime to convert a trade secret to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner, knowing that the offense will injure the owner of that trade secret. The modification allows for the potential of theft of trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned and its Representatives shall under no circumstances contact directly or indirectly any of the residents, partners or former partners or directors of Baldwin: (i) to ask any questions regarding the possible acquisition of all or part of the Assets, (ii) to request additional information or regarding the contents of any of the Information previously obtained, (iii) to request a facility tour or meeting, or (iv) for any other reason , except in the ordinary course of business;
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned and its Representatives shall not contact directly or indirectly any of the current directors of Baldwin to ask questions regarding the possible acquisition of all or part of the Assets, except in the ordinary course of business.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the restriction only to current directors and only to questions regarding the acquisition, while omitting the other categories of prohibited contact, and removing the general prohibition, the clause is weakened. This could create a contradiction with laws protecting business privacy or against tortious interference, as the restriction against contacting former partners is now gone.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario's Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: By significantly narrowing the scope of who cannot be contacted, the modified clause weakens the protection of personal information of former partners and directors. While PIPEDA is primarily focused on commercial activities, this clause's original scope was broader and implicitly protected such information; the revised clause significantly reduces this protection, potentially allowing for inappropriate contact and information gathering contrary to PIPEDA's spirit of protecting personal information.

perturbation.justification: The original text broadly prohibited contacting residents, partners, former partners, or directors for various reasons related to the acquisition. The modified text significantly narrows this restriction to only current directors and only regarding questions about the acquisition. This omission weakens the protection of the personal information of former partners and directors, potentially allowing for inappropriate contact and information gathering. The second scraped law snippet references PIPEDA and its aim to protect personal information, and the modified text weakens the original text's broader protection of such information, creating a contradiction. The omission removes a layer of protection initially present.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned shall indemnify BDO , Baldwin and their representatives against any losses, cost s, damage s, expense s or liabilities suffered or incurred by any of them as a result of or in connection with any breach by the undersigned or any of its Representatives to whom the undersigned discloses Information of any term or provision of this NDA; prov ided, however, that the undersigned shall not be liable for any consequential, special, incidental, indirect or penal damages;
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned shall indemnify BDO , Baldwin and their representatives against any direct losses suffered or incurred by any of them as a result of a breach by the undersigned of any term of this NDA.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the indemnification to 'direct losses' and removing the liability for 'costs, damages, expenses, or liabilities' stemming from the representatives' breaches, the scope of protection is greatly reduced. This creates a potential contradiction with legal standards requiring broader indemnification for breaches of confidentiality, as some laws require coverage for all reasonably foreseeable damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3333 states that for breach of an obligation not arising from contract, the measure of damages is the amount which will compensate for all detriment proximately caused thereby, whether it could have been anticipated or not. By limiting indemnification to only 'direct losses' and excluding coverage for 'costs, damages, expenses, or liabilities', the modified clause potentially contradicts California law by not fully compensating the injured parties for all foreseeable losses resulting from a breach of the confidentiality agreement, particularly if the breach is considered outside the realm of pure contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3333. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 2. Damages for Wrongs [3333 - 3343.7] ( Article 2 enacted 1872. ) 3333. For the breach of an obligation not arising from contract, the measure of damages, except where otherwise expressly provided by this Code, is the amount which will compensate for all the detriment proximately caused thereby, whether it could have been anticipated or not. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language, by limiting indemnification to 'direct losses' and removing liability for 'costs, damages, expenses, or liabilities', potentially contradicts California Civil Code § 3333. This law states that for breaches not arising from contract, damages should compensate for 'all the detriment proximately caused thereby, whether it could have been anticipated or not.' The original text provided broader indemnification, while the changed text narrows it significantly. This creates a contradiction because it may not fully compensate for all foreseeable losses resulting from a breach, especially if the breach is considered outside of pure contract law as stated in snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned acknowledges and agrees that it has had an opportunity to obtain independent legal advice as to the terms and conditions of this NDA and has either received same or expr essly waived its right to do so;
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned acknowledges and agrees that it has reviewed the terms and conditions of this NDA.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the acknowledgment of the opportunity to seek independent legal advice and the waiver of that right weakens the enforceability of the NDA. This is particularly problematic because it might contradict requirements under contract law or consumer protection laws that require disclosures to be understood freely, voluntarily, and with full knowledge of the implications.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Quebec Consumer Protection Act, CQLR c P-40.1, s 103
perturbation.law_explanation: While this NDA is governed by Ontario law, Baldwin Paper Co. Limited/Papiers Baldwin Compagine Limitee is a Quebec company. If the counterparty is a consumer residing in Quebec, Section 103 of the Quebec Consumer Protection Act requires contracts to be drafted clearly. By removing the acknowledgment of the opportunity to seek independent legal advice, there is a higher risk that a consumer might claim they did not fully understand the NDA's implications, potentially allowing them to argue that the contract is abusive or unenforceable under Quebec law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: THE CONSTITUTION ACTS 1867 to 1982 Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu CONSTITUTION ACT, 1982 End note (81) PART I Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms Â Whereas Canada is founded upon principles that recognize the supremacy of God and the rule of law: Guarantee of Rights and Freedoms Marginal note: Rights and freedoms in Canada 1 Â The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms guarantees the rights and freedoms set out in it subject only to such reasonable limits prescribed by law as can be demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society. Fundamental Freedoms Marginal note: Fundamental freedoms 2 Â Everyone has the following fundamental freedoms: (a) Â freedom of conscience and religion; (b) Â freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression, including freedom of the press and other media of communication; (c) Â freedom of peaceful assembly; and (d) Â freedom of association. Democratic Rights Marginal note: Democratic rights of citizens 3 Â Every citizen of Canada has the right to vote in an election of members of the House of Commons or of a legislative assembly and to be qualified for membership therein. Marginal note: Maximum duration of legislative bodies 4 Â (1) Â No House of Commons and no legislative assembly shall continue for longer than five years from the date fixed for the return of the writs of a general election of its members. End note (82) Marginal note: Continuation in special circumstances (2) Â In time of real or apprehended war, invasion or insurrection, a House of Commons may be continued by Parliament and a legislative assembly may be continued by the legislature beyond five years if such continuation is not opposed by the votes of more than one-third of the members of the House of Commons or the legislative assembly, as the case may be. End note (83) Marginal note: Annual sitting of legislative bodies 5 Â There shall be a sitting of Parliament and of each legislature at least once every twelve months. End note (84) Mobility Rights Marginal note: Mobility of citizens 6 Â (1) Â Every citizen of Canada has the right to enter, remain in and leave Canada. Marginal note: Rights to move and gain livelihood (2) Â Every citizen of Canada and every person who has the status of a permanent resident of Canada has the right (a) Â to move to and take up residence in any province; and (b) Â to pursue the gaining of a livelihood in any province. Marginal note: Limitation (3) Â The rights specified in subsection (2) are subject to (a) Â any laws or practices of general application in force in a province other than those that discriminate among persons primarily on the basis of province of present or previous residence; and (b) Â any laws providing for reasonable residency requirements as a qualification for the receipt of publicly provided social services. Marginal note: Affirmative action programs (4) Â Subsections (2) and (3) do not preclude any law, program or activity that has as its object the amelioration in a province of conditions of individuals in that province who are socially or economically disadvantaged if the rate of employment in that province is below the rate of employment in Canada. Legal Rights Marginal note: Life, liberty and security of person 7 Â Everyone has the right to life, liberty and security of the person and the right not to be deprived thereof except in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice. Marginal note: Search or seizure 8 Â Everyone has the right to be secure against unreasonable search or seizure. Marginal note: Detention or imprisonment 9 Â Everyone has the right not to be arbitrarily detained or imprisoned. Marginal note: Arrest or detention 10 Â Everyone has the right on arrest or detention (a) Â to be informed promptly of the reasons therefor; (b) Â to retain and instruct counsel without delay and to be informed of that right; and (c) Â to have the validity of the detention determined by way of habeas corpus and to be rele
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures the signer acknowledges the opportunity for independent legal advice or waives that right. The changed text omits this acknowledgment, weakening the enforceability, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation suggests that under Quebec consumer protection laws, this omission could lead to a consumer claiming they did not fully understand the NDA's implications. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 from the Constitution Act does not directly address contract law, the removal of the acknowledgment directly relates to the understanding and voluntary nature of the agreement, aligning with consumer protection principles. This makes the contract potentially unenforceable, creating a significant legal contradiction.
